ontology.foodex2_description: Canned fruits — processed fruit preserved in syrup or juice
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for generic canned fruit in syrup; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of canned fruits is approximately 120 g (about half a cup of drained fruit pieces), as commonly consumed as a snack or dessert accompaniment.

nutrient_source.title: Canned fruits — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F04030001 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are preserved from the HPB source. Sugar_g is estimated at 15.5 g/100g by subtracting dietary fibre (1.7 g) from total carbohydrate (18.8 g) and applying a typical syrup-packed fruit sugar fraction consistent with HPB and USDA FoodData Central profiles for canned fruit in heavy syrup. Saturated fat is estimated at 0.02 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for canned fruit cocktail in heavy syrup (FDC ID 168155), consistent with the negligible total fat of 0.1 g/100g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for generic canned mixed fruit in syrup in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated using canned peaches in heavy syrup (GI ~58, University of Sydney GI Database) and canned fruit cocktail in syrup (GI ~55, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) as the closest analogous approved-source entries. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per serving (18.8 g/100g × 1.2 serving factor × 0.876 available fraction) / 100 = approximately 10.2 g GL per 120 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar content (~15.5 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.7 g/100g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention despite low sodium (6 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should choose canned fruits packed in water or natural juice (no added sugar) and drain syrup before consumption; syrup-packed varieties deliver ~15.5 g sugar/100g which drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and should be limited to occasional small portions of ≤80 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated sugar ~15.5 g/100g with dietary fibre only 1.7 g/100g produces a rapid postprandial glucose spike; GI 55 with high sugar density elevates glycaemic load significantly above fresh fruit
type2_diabetes.guidance: Canned fruits in syrup are not recommended for Type 2 Diabetes management due to estimated sugar of 15.5 g/100g and low fibre (1.7 g/100g); if consumed, limit to ≤80 g portions of water-packed or juice-packed varieties and pair with protein or healthy fat to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat (0.1 g/100g) and low sodium (6 mg/100g) are favourable, but high added sugar (~15.5 g/100g) raises triglycerides and contributes to cardiometabolic risk with regular consumption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While canned fruits in syrup are low in fat and sodium, the high added sugar content (~15.5 g/100g) can elevate serum triglycerides with habitual intake; cardiovascular patients should prefer fresh fruit or water-packed canned fruit and limit syrup-packed varieties to occasional use.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar (~15.5 g/100g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators relevant to RA despite fruit-derived antioxidants
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid syrup-packed canned fruits due to high added sugar content (~15.5 g/100g) which promotes inflammatory mediators; water-packed or fresh fruit alternatives provide antioxidant benefits without the inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Canned fruits in syrup are preserved fruit pieces — typically a mixture of peaches, pears, grapes, cherries, and pineapple — that have been heat-sterilised and packed in sugar syrup inside sealed metal or glass containers. The canning process softens the fruit texture, making it tender and juicy, with a sweet flavour profile dominated by the added syrup rather than the natural fruit sugars. The product is shelf-stable for one to three years without refrigeration. Per 100 g, canned fruits in syrup provide approximately 70 kcal, 18.8 g carbohydrate (of which an estimated 15.5 g is sugar), 0.47 g protein, 0.1 g fat, 1.7 g dietary fibre, and only 6 mg sodium. The high sugar content from the syrup significantly elevates the glycaemic load compared to fresh fruit equivalents, making this product unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension without modification. Clinically, the combination of ~15.5 g sugar/100g and low fibre (1.7 g/100g) produces a rapid postprandial glucose response. Choosing water-packed or juice-packed alternatives reduces sugar by approximately 30–50%. The product is plant-based, gluten-free, dairy-free, and low in sodium, making it broadly suitable for general populations in moderation. It is commonly consumed as a snack, dessert topping, or ingredient in fruit salads and trifles across Singapore and globally.
common_user_queries: Canned fruit vs fresh fruit nutrition
Is canned fruit in syrup bad for you?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F04030001, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Sugar_g estimated from carbohydrate and fibre values consistent with HPB and USDA FoodData Central profiles for canned fruit in syrup. GI estimated from closest analogous approved-source entries (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008). All values are internally consistent and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168155.
notes: Sugar_g (15.5 g/100g) is estimated by subtracting dietary fibre (1.7 g) from total carbohydrate (18.8 g) and applying a typical syrup-packed fruit sugar fraction; this is consistent with USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168155 (fruit cocktail, canned, heavy syrup) which reports 15.6 g sugar/100g. Saturated_fat_g (0.02 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.02 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.03 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168155 given total fat of 0.1 g/100g; these are trace values consistent with the negligible fat content. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible for plant-based canned fruit products per USDA FoodData Central. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food product with no animal-derived ingredients. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food. Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed negligible for canned fruit per USDA FoodData Central. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on canned peaches in heavy syrup and canned fruit cocktail in syrup from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI entry exists for generic canned mixed fruit in syrup. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168155 as the closest approved-source equivalent; these are not reported in the HPB source record.
